content.full_text:
Re
Fern PolrrrcAt. PRACrrcEs C ouvrrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 e Sacramenro, CA g5gl4-232g
(9t6) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_0886
January 19,2010
Thomas A. Willis
Remcho, Johansen & Purcell, LLp
201 Delores Avenue
San Leandro, CA 94577
Your Request for lnformal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-180
Dear Mr. Willis:
This letter responds to your requ,est for advice regarding the campaign finance provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r This letter is based oi the fa"ts pr!.ented. The Fair
Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it
renders assistance. (ln re oglesby (I975) I FPPC ops. 71.) Because your question is general in
nature and you have not provided the identity of the person on whose behaf you have irade yo*r
request, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other laws that may apply.
QUESTTON
Does the Act prohibit contributions, expenditures, or independent expenditures in
connection with a ballot measure by a trust established under the laws of a state of the United
States if the trust is settled by a foreign trust acting at the direction of a United States citizen?
CONCLUSION
Under the specific facts you have provided, the citizenof the United States directing the
foreign trust is the source of any contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure maJe by
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections g 1000 through g l014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
commission are contained in Sections I 8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regglations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 831 14, Regularion 19329(cX j).)


File No. I-10-180
Page No.2
the trust established under the laws of a state of the United States. Thus, contributions,
expenditures, or independent expenditures in connection with a ballot measure by the trust
established under the laws of a state of the United States are not prohibited.
FACTS
You have asked for reconsideration of the Wtllis Advice Letter, No. I-10-146 in light of
additional facts you have provided. As more fully detailed in our p."uiom letter, you seek
assistance on behalf of a trust established in a foriign country (the "Foreign Tmst,,) that intends
to settle a second trust under the laws of a state of the United States 1th. .{;.S. Trust,,). once
settled, the U.S. Trust would like to use a portion of its funds to support ballot measures in the
state of Califomia to the extent that the measures are compatible wiih its goal of promoting long-
tenn governmental reform. The U.S. Trust will not make any contributions to or expenditures
for or against any federal, state, or local candidates.
As you have described in your request for reconsideration, dated October 2l, ZOlO,and a
telephone conversation on November 17,-2010, the Foreign Trust was formed in 1gg3 by person
B solely for the benefit of his son ("Person A") and Perso-n A's children or remoter issue, of
which there currently are none. However, Person B's wife and mother of person A (the ,.wife,,)
and a foreign corporation (the "Foreign Coqporation") fully controlled by another foreign trust
(the "Foreign Trust II") were subsequentty aaaeA as beneficiaries of tn" ro."ig1 Trust.
The current beneficiaries of the Foreign Trust II are (l) any charity, (2) the Wife, (3)
Person A's children or remoter issue, and (4)ln hmited circumstances person A,s siblings and
their remoter issue. Person A is the sole protector of the Foreign Trust II and has the limited
power of appointment with respect to the trust that enables him to make distributions from the
trust to any person other than himself, his estate, his creditors, or creditors of his estate.
Neither Person B, nor the Wife, was or is a United States citizen or resident, and the
trustees of the Foreign Trust are also foreign persons. Person A, however, is a united States
citizen' while formed as a revocable and discretionary tmst, person B's power to revoke the
trust during his lifetime was subject to the consent of an independent proiector and the trust
became irrevocable upon Person B's death in2007. Additionally, peison B identified person A
as the "principal beneficiary" of the Foreign Trust in a Memorandum of principles provided to
the trustees in 1997. Though non-binding, you have indicated that the Memorandum of
Principles strongly influences the trusteei' decisions in regards to the distribution and
management of trust funds.
- 
Currently, both Person A and the wife have been removed from the Foreign Trust as 
r
beneficiaries' Person A was removed at his own reqllest in 2008. person B's wife was removed
upon the amendment of the Foreign Trust after the death of person B. Upon being removed as a
beneficiary, Person A was named the sole protector of the Foreign Tmst. As the protector,
Person A recommended that all assets of the Foreign Trust be used for charitable pllrposes.
while not bound by Person A's recommendation, the trustees renamed the Foreign Trust the
"Person A Charitable Trust" in2010. The Foreign Trust is now held for the benefit of charitable


File No. I-10-180
Page No. 3
organizations, including those described in Section 501(c)(3) and (4) of the United States
Intemal Revenue Code, and the Foreign Corporation, which is the only beneficiary other than
charitable organizations.
Person A now intends to recommend to the independent trustees of the Foreign Trust that
a portion of the Foreign Trust be used to irrevocably setile a trust (the "U.S. Trust,,) under the
laws of a state of the United States. As anticipated, Person A will be the .,investment tmstee,, of
the U.S. Trust. A trust company will act as a co-trustee, but will be limited to serving as an
"administrative trustee." In general, the trust company will have custody of trust property;
maintain trust records; and will be responsible for preparing tax retums, the receipt anO
distribution of written notices, and carrying out the dulies and directions of the investment
trustee (Person A).
The U.S. Trust will be categorized as a "United States trust" for federal income tax
purposes. Thus, the U.S. Trust will fulfill the requirements set forth in Section 77Ol(a)(3Ot(E) of
the Internal Revenue Code. As you have presented the relevant portions of Section
7701(a)(30)(E) of the Intemal Revenue Code, a trust will be a "0nited States person,, under the
code if a court within the United States is able to exercise primary supervision over the
administration of the trust and one or more United States pircon, havl the authority to control all
"substantial decisions" of the trust. You state that the Internal Revenue Code defines
"substantial decisions" to include decisions as to (l) whether and when to distribute income and
corpus; (2) the amount of any distribution; (3) the selection of a benefic iary; (4) whether a
receipt is allocable to income or principal; (5) whether to terminate the ,*r,t iO) whether to
compromise, arbitrate, or abandon claims of the trust; (7) whether to sue on behalf of the trust or
to defend suits against the trust; and (8) whether to remove, add, or replace a trustee. (See C.F.R.
Section 301.7701-7(dxlxii).) The U.S. Trust will comply with all fediral and state reporting
requirements applicable to United States trusts.
The U.S. Trust will not conduct any business for profit. As investment trustee, person
A's primary responsibility will be the preservation of truit property, and person A will not be
empowered to operate a business. The only beneficiaries ol ttrl U.S. trust will be charitable
organizations described in Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code and non-profit civic
leagues and organizations described in Section 501(c)(a) of the code. Upon the termination of
the trust, the balanceof any remaining trust funds will be distributed to tlhe designated
beneficiaries (charitable organizations). As part of its efforts and to the extent that the measures
are compatible with its goals of promoting govemmental reform, the U.S. Tmst would like to Llse
part of its funds to support ballot measures in the State of California by contributing to ballot
measure committees established by 501(c)(3) and 501(c)(4) nonprofit organizations.
AIIALYSIS
In pertinent part, Section 95320 provides the following:


File No. I-10-180
Page No.4
"(a) N9 foreign government or foreign principal shall make, directly or
through any other person, any contribution, exienditure, or independent
expenditure in connection with the qualification or support ol oi opposition to,
any state or local ballot measure.
tiu
"(c) For the purposes of this section, a "foreign principal" includes the
following:
"(l) A foreign political parry.
"(2) A person outside the United States, unless either of the following is
established:
"(A) The person is an individual and a citizen of the United States.
"(B) The person is not an individual and is organized under or created by
the laws of the United States or of any state or other flace subject to the
jurisdiction of the United states and has its principal ptace of iusiness within the
United States.
"(3) A partnership, association, corporation, organi zation,or other
combination of persons organized under thi laws of oihaving its'p.incipal place
of business in a foreign country.
"(4) A domestic subsidiary of a foreign corporation if the decision to
contribute or expend funds is made by an officer, d:irector, or management
employee of the foreign corporation who is neither a citizenof the United States
nor a lawftrlly admitted permanent resident of the united States.,,
As analyzed in our prior letter, it is clear that the foreign trust is a foreign principal
pursuant to section 85320(c)(3) and Section 85320(a) prohibiis a foreign principai from making
any contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure in connection;ith the qualification or
support of, or opposition to, any state or local bailot measure either directly or thro,ghanother
person' Nonetheless, as provided by Section 85320(c)(2XB), it appears that the U.S. Trust is not
a foreign principal. Thus, the critical determination is whether the foreign trust is making a
contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure through the U.S. irust under the
circurnstance3 you have presented.
In general, the_source of funds provided by a foreign trust to settle a trust established
under the laws of the united states is the foreign imst animerely passing funds through another
trust does not impact our analysis of the solrrce of the funds. However, *a., the facts you have
provided, we cannot dismiss the significance of the following:


File No. I-10-180
Page No. 5
o The Foreign Trust was originally established in 1993 by Person B solely for the benefit of
Person A and Person A's children or remoter issue, of which there currently are none.
Person A is no longer a beneficiary of the Foreign Tmst only because of his own
voluntary actions in removing himself from the trust.
o The trustees of the Foreign Tmst are highly influenced by a Memorandum of Principles
provided to the trustees in 1997, in which Person B identified Person A as the "prin-ipal
beneficiary" of the Foreign Trust.
o It is Person A's recommendation to the trustees of the Foreign Trust that a portion of the
Foreign Trust be used to irrevocably settle U.S. Trust for charitable purposes.
o Person A will serve as the "investment trustee" of the U.S. Trust and will have the
authority to control all "substantial decisions" of the trust.
Should the trustees of the Foreign Trust ultimately accept Person A's recommendation to
settle the U.S. Trust and name Person A as the investment trustee of the trust, it appears that the
trustees of the Foreign Trust are acting on the behalf of and at the direction of Person A for all
relevant pllrposes, and in accordance with the Memorandum of Principles, despite the fact that
Person A is no longer a named beneficiary of the Foreign Trust. ln addition, the fact that the
Foreign Trust was originally settled in 1993 by the father of Person A, for the sole benefit of
Person A and his children or remoter issue, alleviates the concern that the proposal to establish
the U.S. Trust was derived with the nefarious intention of circumventing Section 55320. Based
upon the additional facts you have provided, we find that Person A should be considered the
source of the funds provided by the Foreign Trust to settle the U.S. Trust. Accordingly, the use
of these funds for the support of a ballot measure is not prohibited under Section 8S3ZO.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G
Counsel, Legal Division

foreign trust is the source of any contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure maJe by
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 831 14, Regularion 19329(cX j).)
the trust established under the laws of a state of the United States. Thus, contributions,
expenditures, or independent expenditures in connection with a ballot measure by the trust
established under the laws of a state of the United States are not prohibited.
